title: Cordero al chilindron
subtitle: Aragonese lamb with peppers, tomatoes, and serrano ham
excerpt: Lamb shoulder pieces browned and then braised in a sauce of roasted red peppers, tomatoes, onions, garlic, and strips of serrano ham. A rustic dish from Aragon in north-east Spain, with a sauce that is deeply sweet and savoury from the peppers and slightly smoky from the ham.
sourceNotes: Chilindron is a sauce native to Aragon and Navarre in north-eastern Spain, made from roasted red peppers, tomatoes, onions, and ham. It appears in nineteenth-century Spanish cookbooks applied to lamb, chicken, and rabbit. The name may derive from the Spanish card game 'chillindron', though the etymology is disputed. It is one of the signature dishes of Aragonese cooking.

content.text: The pepper is the foundation of the chilindron sauce. Use red peppers: they are sweeter and break down more completely than green, giving the sauce a deep terracotta colour and a rich, slightly sweet character that balances the fattiness of the lamb. The serrano ham provides the salt and a faint smoke note; it is added early so it has time to meld with the other flavours rather than sitting on top as a garnish.

Lamb shoulder is the right cut for this braise. It has enough fat and collagen to stay moist through 90 minutes of cooking and releases the collagen into the sauce as it braises, enriching and thickening it without any flour. Lamb neck is equally suitable.
Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} pieces well. Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a casserole over high heat. Brown the lamb in batches, 3 minutes per side, until deeply coloured. Remove and set aside.

Lower the heat to medium. Add the {{serrano-ham}} strips and cook for 2 minutes until beginning to crisp. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} and {{pepper-red}} and cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally. Stir in the {{paprika-smoked}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{thyme}}, and {{bay-leaves}}.

Return the lamb to the casserole, nestling it into the sauce. The sauce should come about halfway up the lamb. Cover and cook over low heat for 75 to 90 minutes until the lamb is very tender and the sauce has thickened around it. Remove the thyme and bay leaves. Taste and adjust seasoning, the serrano ham provides most of the salt. Serve with crusty bread or boiled potatoes.

items.fix: Add a small pinch of sugar; cook the peppers for the full 5 minutes before adding wine. They need time to soften and sweeten
items.cause: Peppers not cooked long enough, or tomatoes too sharp

items.fix: Cook the paprika in the fat for 1 minute only, then add wine straight away; paprika burns fast
Braise for another 30 minutes at a low simmer; lamb shoulder releases collagen slowly and needs time
items.cause: Temperature too high, the braise was boiling rather than simmering

items.fix: Add the ham strips in small quantities and taste, some serrano is much saltier than others; adjust before adding more
items.cause: Too much serrano or used a very cured/salty variety

content.text: Chilindron is the defining sauce of Aragon, the way bouillabaisse belongs to Marseille. Aragon is semi-arid sheep country, and the dish uses what the landscape produces: lamb, peppers, and the cured ham made in the Pyrenean foothills. The sauce works just as well with chicken thighs or rabbit. In Aragonese households it is a Sunday lunch, served straight from the casserole.
